local_names.malay: Teh C Peng / Teh Tiga Lapis

aliases: Iced Evaporated Milk Tea with Palm Sugar

english: Iced Evaporated Milk Tea with Palm Sugar

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Teh C Peng or three-layer iced tea. Mapped to the broader 'tea beverage' class (FOODON_03301737) as the closest defensible ancestor. A more specific term for milk tea with palm sugar layering does not exist in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FHR (Tea-based beverages) used as the closest defensible category. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for Teh C Peng or layered evaporated milk tea with palm sugar.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific beverage preparation. SNOMED CT does not have a term for Teh C Peng or three-layer iced tea as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of Teh C Peng is approximately 250 ml (one glass), which is the typical portion served at Malaysian kopitiams and hawker stalls.

nutrient_source.title: Energy and Nutrient Composition of Foods — HPB Singapore / Malaysian Beverage Composition Data
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) and IMR Malaysia MyFCD
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved source entry exists for Teh C Peng (Three-Layer Iced Tea) as a composite beverage. Values are adapted from HPB Singapore beverage composition data for evaporated milk tea and IMR Malaysia MyFCD data for palm sugar (gula melaka) syrup and black tea. The per-100g values represent a blended estimate for the standard three-layer preparation (approximately 40% black tea, 35% evaporated milk, 25% palm sugar syrup by volume). Input AI-generated values were cross-checked against these approved sources and retained where consistent. Saturated fat estimated from evaporated full-cream milk composition per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 01097).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; and University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for sweetened milk tea
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Teh C Peng (Three-Layer Iced Tea) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 60 (medium) is estimated by analogy to sweetened evaporated milk tea with added sugar/syrup, referencing University of Sydney GI Database entries for sweetened tea beverages (GI ~55–65) and the moderating effect of fat and protein from evaporated milk on the glycemic response to palm sugar syrup. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (60 × 30.5) / 100 = 18.3 (250g serving × 12.2g carbohydrate per 100g = 30.5g carbohydrate per serving).

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (45 mg per 100g); caffeine from black tea may transiently raise blood pressure
hypertension.guidance: Teh C Peng is low in sodium (approximately 113 mg per 250 ml serving) and poses minimal direct hypertensive risk from salt; however, individuals with hypertension should be aware that caffeine in black tea may cause a transient blood pressure rise, and the high sugar content contributes to overall cardiometabolic load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar content (11.5g per 100g; approximately 28.8g per 250ml serving) from palm sugar syrup drives significant postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit Teh C Peng due to its high free sugar content (~28.8g per serving) from palm sugar syrup, which produces a medium-to-high glycemic load (GL ~18) per serving and can cause significant postprandial hyperglycaemia; a sugar-free or reduced-sugar version with less palm sugar syrup is strongly recommended.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from evaporated full-cream milk (~1.6g per 100g) and high free sugar content contribute to atherogenic and metabolic risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Teh C Peng contains saturated fat from evaporated milk and a high free sugar load per serving; regular consumption may contribute to elevated LDL cholesterol and triglycerides, and individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit intake or request a reduced-sugar, lower-fat preparation using low-fat evaporated milk.

caffeine_sensitivity.key_factor: Black tea contributes approximately 20–40 mg caffeine per 250 ml serving
caffeine_sensitivity.guidance: Individuals sensitive to caffeine should consume Teh C Peng in moderation, particularly in the afternoon or evening, as the black tea base contributes a moderate caffeine dose that may disrupt sleep or cause palpitations in sensitive individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Teh C Peng, also known as Three-Layer Tea (Teh Tiga Lapis), is a visually striking and iconic iced tea beverage originating from Sarawak, Malaysia, and widely popular across Malaysian kopitiams and hawker centres. The drink is composed of three distinct visible layers served in a tall glass over ice: a dark, rich palm sugar syrup (gula melaka) at the bottom, creamy evaporated full-cream milk (commonly Carnation brand, hence the 'C' in the name) in the middle, and a strong brew of black tea poured carefully on top. The word 'Peng' means ice in Hokkien, indicating the drink is served chilled. Before drinking, the layers are stirred together to produce a beverage with a complex flavour profile: deep caramel sweetness from the palm sugar, a rich creaminess from the evaporated milk, and the robust, slightly astringent character of strong black tea. The texture is smooth and milky when mixed. Nutritionally, Teh C Peng provides approximately 75 kcal per 100g (187 kcal per 250 ml serving), with 1.5g protein, 2.5g fat (predominantly saturated from evaporated milk), and 12.2g carbohydrate per 100g — of which 11.5g is sugar, primarily from palm sugar syrup. The beverage has a medium glycemic index (~60) and a glycemic load of approximately 18 per serving, making it a high-sugar drink that requires caution for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease. It is low in sodium and provides small amounts of calcium, potassium, and B vitamins from the evaporated milk component. Teh C Peng is best enjoyed as an occasional refreshing treat and is a culturally significant beverage representing the kopitiam (traditional coffee shop) culture of Malaysia and Singapore.

anytime iced drink in tropical climate

confidence_reason: No single approved source entry exists for Teh C Peng as a composite beverage. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore beverage composition data and IMR Malaysia MyFCD data for the component ingredients (evaporated milk, palm sugar syrup, black tea), cross-checked against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for evaporated milk composition. The GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for sweetened milk tea beverages. Confidence is medium because the values are compositionally derived rather than directly measured for this specific preparation.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted estimates for the standard Teh C Peng preparation (approximately 40% black tea, 35% evaporated milk, 25% palm sugar syrup by volume) based on HPB Singapore and IMR Malaysia MyFCD component data. No single approved source entry exists for this composite beverage as a whole. 2. Saturated fat (1.6g/100g), monounsaturated_fat_g (0.7g/100g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.1g/100g), and cholesterol (8.0mg/100g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy evaporated milk data (NDB 01097) proportioned to the beverage composition. 3. Water content (82.0g/100g) is estimated from the liquid nature of the beverage and component proportions. 4. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 — confirmed negligible/trace in evaporated milk and palm sugar syrup per USDA FoodData Central. 5. Dietary fibre is 0.0g/100g — naturally zero for this liquid beverage with no solid plant matter retained after brewing and straining. 6. GI of 60 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Teh C Peng exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2024. 7. The input source citation ('Nutritional Value of Popular Malaysian Beverages', HPB Singapore) was retained as a supporting reference; however, the specific URL https://www.hpb.gov.sg/article/nutritional-value-of-popular-malaysian-beverages could not be independently verified as a discrete publication — HPB Singapore is an approved source and the general domain is valid. 8. Tamil and Hindi local names are not applicable for this Malaysian/Sarawakian kopitiam beverage and have been left null. 9. Glycemic load per serving calculated as GL = (60 × 30.5) / 100 = 18.3, where 30.5g is the carbohydrate content of a 250g serving (250 × 0.122).
